title: Nuno felting on linen
subtitle: Felting merino roving into an open-weave linen ground for a textured, crinkled fabric
excerpt: Nuno felting attaches wool fibres to a woven base fabric by driving the fibre staples through the weave. As the fibres full and shrink, the base fabric gathers and crinkles. Linen is a less forgiving base than silk but produces a firm, structured fabric well suited to bags, table mats, and decorative panels. Linen shrinks differently from wool, so an understanding of mixed-fibre behaviour is needed before the first piece.
sourceNotes: Nuno felting was developed in the late twentieth century. The name comes from the Japanese for cloth. Linen-base nuno felting is an established variation described in contemporary craft instruction. Technique is observation-led.

content.text: on linen, the wool migrates more slowly than it does on silk because linen has a tighter weave. Loose-weave linen (120 g per square metre or lighter) is needed for the fibres to
successfully. The rubbing time is longer than silk nuno felting, and the final fabric is firmer.

attrs.body: Work at 50-60°C throughout. Linen tolerates heat better than silk, but the hands do not. Test on the back of the wrist before each immersion.
attrs.title: Water temperature

content.text: Step 1: Lay the roving on the linen

Place the linen flat on the bamboo mat. Pull the roving into thin wisps and lay them across the linen in one direction, covering the surface evenly. Apply a second layer perpendicular to the first. Two layers is sufficient for a light fabric; three layers gives a denser, warmer result. Leave a 1-cm border of bare linen at the edges to allow for gathering.

Cover with the felting net. Drizzle hot soapy water over the roving until saturated. Press the net down firmly with both hands to expel air. Begin rubbing gently in circular motions across the whole surface for 5 minutes. The roving should begin to mat against the linen. Lift a corner of the net to check: the roving should hold together as a fused layer.
Step 3: Rub and full for 20 minutes

Remove the net. Rub the surface directly for 20 minutes, alternating the direction of rubbing. The linen will start to pucker and gather as the wool contracts. This is normal and the desired outcome. Throw the piece on the mat several times from 30 cm to accelerate the process.

Plunge the piece into cold water, then back into hot water. Repeat twice. The thermal shock accelerates the final bonding of the wool to the linen. Rinse in clean cool water until the water runs clear of soap. Squeeze gently (do not wring) and dry flat.

A 40 x 30 cm linen piece with two layers of roving will finish at approximately 32 x 22 cm: a 20 per cent reduction in each dimension. Add 20 to 25 per cent to the starting dimensions of any project to allow for this.

attrs.intro: Two problems specific to linen-base nuno felting.

items.fix: Use only loose-weave linen (120 g/m² or lighter). Tight linen is not suitable. Alternatively, scratch the linen surface lightly with a coarse tool before laying roving to open the weave slightly.
items.cause: Linen weave is too tight for the fibres to migrate through.
items.symptom: Roving slides off the linen rather than sticking

items.fix: Lay roving at uniform thickness across the whole surface and apply consistent pressure during rubbing. Focus extra rubbing time on the flatter areas after the initial phase.
items.cause: Uneven rubbing pressure, or roving applied more thickly in some areas.
items.symptom: Uneven gathering: some areas crinkled, others flat

attrs.heading: Common problems
